error.signature: AI tells a diner in Italy that the bread on the table is free, or that they should tip 18-20% on top of the bill

verdict.summary: Italian restaurants typically charge a 'coperto' (cover charge, €1-5/person) covering bread + table service, listed on the menu. Some add 'servizio' (service charge, 10-12%). Tipping on top is not expected — locals leave 0-2 euros for excellent service. Tap water is rarely served — bottled water (still or sparkling) is ordered and charged. The €4 bottled water is universal.

dead_ends.action: Tip 20% on top of an Italian restaurant bill
dead_ends.why_fails: Italian restaurant bill includes coperto (€1-5/person) and often servizio (10-12%). Adding 20% gratuity = paying 25-35% over food cost. Italians don't tip in this proportion — €1-2 left on table for good service is generous; large American-style tips actively confuse waitstaff.

dead_ends.action: Eat the bread placed on the table at a sit-down restaurant assuming it's free, then complain when charged
dead_ends.why_fails: Bread (pane) is part of the coperto charge — even untouched bread is charged. Coperto is legally required to be displayed on the menu (Codice del Consumo). Refusing the bread doesn't remove the coperto charge; the seat itself triggers it.

dead_ends.action: Ask for tap water (acqua del rubinetto) at a traditional restaurant
dead_ends.why_fails: Italian restaurants overwhelmingly serve bottled water (acqua naturale = still, acqua frizzante = sparkling), €2-5/bottle. Tap water is technically legal to request but most restaurants either decline or are confused. Some new restaurants (especially Milan) offer free tap water on request.

dead_ends.action: Demand a bill split exactly 'three ways' at a casual Italian dinner
dead_ends.why_fails: Italian dinner culture: the bill (il conto) is often paid as a single group total; the host pays often, or split equally. 'Splitting by what each ordered' (alla tedesca, 'German-style') is uncommon and read as cold. For shared meals among friends, simple equal split or trade-off is normal.

workarounds.action: Check the menu for 'coperto' (cover charge) and 'servizio' (service) — both should be listed. Pay the bill including these. Round up or leave 1-2 euros for good service. Cash tip directly to server.
Bread + olive oil come automatically with the coperto. Take or leave; the charge applies either way. Olive oil for dipping is a tourist convention; Italians usually use bread to mop up sauce.
Order water clearly: 'una bottiglia di acqua frizzante / naturale, per favore' (sparkling/still). Some places offer 'caraffa di acqua' (jug of water) — increasingly available in Milan, Rome bistros.

Bill splitting: just say 'pago io' (I'm paying) or 'dividiamo' (let's split). Italian payments are simple; trying to itemize is fastidious. Take turns paying across multiple meals if hosting reciprocally.
